elements.sent-2411-001: Automating complex industrial robots requires precise nonlinear control and efficient energy management.
elements.sent-2411-002: This paper introduces a data-driven nonlinear model predictive control (NMPC) framework to optimize control under multiple objectives.
elements.sent-2411-003: To enhance the prediction accuracy of the dynamic model, we design a single-shot multi-step prediction (SSMP) model based on long short-term memory (LSTM) and multilayer perceptrons (MLP), which can directly obtain the predictive horizon without iterative repetition and reduce computational pressure.
elements.sent-2411-004: Moreover, we combine offline and online models to address disturbances stemming from environmental interactions, similar to the superposition of the robot's free and forced responses.
elements.sent-2411-005: The online model learns the system's variations from the prediction mismatches of the offline model and updates its weights in real time.
elements.sent-2411-006: The proposed hybrid predictive model simplifies the relationship between inputs and outputs into matrix multiplication, which can quickly obtain the derivative.
elements.sent-2411-007: Therefore, the solution for the control signal sequence employs a gradient descent method with an adaptive learning rate, allowing the NMPC cost function to be formulated as a convex function incorporating critical states.
elements.sent-2411-008: The learning rate is dynamically adjusted based on state errors to counteract the inherent prediction inaccuracies of neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-009: The controller outputs the average value of the control signal sequence instead of the first value.
elements.sent-2411-010: Simulations and experiments on a 22-ton hydraulic excavator have validated the effectiveness of our method, showing that the proposed NMPC approach can be widely applied to industrial systems, including nonlinear control and energy management.
elements.sent-2411-011: Most actuating components in these systems are manipulators or other mechatronic-hydraulic products.
elements.sent-2411-012: Achieving high-precision control over these robots is vital for successfully executing engineering tasks.
elements.sent-2411-013: Several traditional methods are commonly used for process and robotic control, including Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) control \cite{malki1997fuzzy ,\cite{premkumar2015fuzzy ,\cite{van2020self and model-based control \cite{kim2019modeling ,\cite{yao2017active ,\cite{williams2017information ,\cite{nguyen2020global .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: PID control is widely adopted due to its straightforward principles and broad applicability, meeting the essential requirements of many industrial applications.
elements.sent-2411-015: However, it has limitations when applied to systems with complex nonlinearities or when optimization is necessary, such as in high-order time-delay systems or resource allocation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-016: Model-based control methods, particularly Model Predictive Control (MPC), are well-known for achieving optimal control in nonlinear systems \cite{dai2020robust ,\cite{di2013stochastic .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: MPC uses a dynamic model to predict system behavior over a future time horizon at each sampling interval and calculates a sequence of optimal control signals to enhance system performance \cite{shen2017trajectory .}
elements.sent-2411-018: The effectiveness of MPC relies on the accuracy of the system's dynamics model, which presents considerable challenges.
elements.sent-2411-019: Nonlinear factors such as combustion \cite{van2001modeling , friction \cite{xiao2021sensorless , and fluid dynamics \cite{gholizadeh2014modeling are difficult to measure directly and often lack precise physical representations.}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Additionally, the dynamics model of a robot may change when it interacts with its external environment.
elements.sent-2411-021: Typical scenarios include handling different loads or the increasing temperature of the robot's drive motors or hydraulic oil \cite{gao2018oscillatory .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Moreover, the receding horizon approach in MPC and complex dynamics may lead to myopic predictions \cite{mitsioni2023safe , potentially resulting in control failures.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Data-driven approaches provide a new perspective for modeling and controlling robotic systems.
elements.sent-2411-024: This method primarily involves using neural networks or Gaussian process regression (GPR) to learn both forward and inverse dynamics, thus avoiding the inaccuracies commonly associated with direct physical modeling.
elements.sent-2411-025: Inverse dynamics \cite{kim2021force can be used for direct control or feedforward compensation, while forward dynamics is crucial in model reference adaptive control (MRAC) or MPC \cite{cui2022approximate ,\cite{wang2020parameter .}}}
elements.sent-2411-026: The computational complexity of GPR is influenced by the training set due to the non-parametric nature, necessitating additional preprocessing to select an appropriate subset of data \cite{ambikasaran2015fast .}
elements.sent-2411-027: In contrast, neural networks are generally more intuitive and user-friendly, which has led to their widespread adoption in the field.
elements.sent-2411-028: A data-driven nonlinear model predictive control (DD-NMPC) approach combines neural network learning models with MPC.
elements.sent-2411-029: By leveraging the universal approximation properties of neural networks, it is possible to learn the dynamics of the control plant either offline or online, resulting in a highly predictive nonlinear model that can be used to optimize the control sequence.
elements.sent-2411-030: However, DD-NMPC also encounters several challenges as the follows.
elements.sent-2411-031: Accurate predictive modeling: The effectiveness of predictive models relies not only on the extensive collection of data but is also heavily influenced by the design of the neural network.
elements.sent-2411-032: For example, recurrent neural networks (RNNs) are more effective than multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) for predicting time-series data because they can capture temporal dependencies \cite{huang2022lstm .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Additionally, the predictive horizon in NMPC needs to be updated continuously through iterations, which can be affected by fitting errors in the neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-034: As prediction errors accumulate and amplify over multiple iterations, they can ultimately reduce the control system's effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-035: Environmental Interaction: The dynamics of a robot can change when it interacts with the external environment, which may reduce the prediction accuracy of a learning model that has been trained on offline data.
elements.sent-2411-036: Online or active learning methods aim to improve offline learning by continuously updating the model with new data collected during operation \cite{saviolo2023active .}
elements.sent-2411-037: However, this iterative process can be time-consuming and risky, and the potential for non-convergence can make it impractical to implement in engineering tasks.
elements.sent-2411-038: Real-time Optimization: In linear MPC, determining control actions is addressed as a quadratic programming (QP) or sequential quadratic programming (SQP) problem.
elements.sent-2411-039: This involves using a quadratic cost function, which efficiently identifies either the optimal or a locally optimal solution.
elements.sent-2411-040: On the other hand, when working with nonlinear models, each iteration requires solving a nonlinear programming (NLP) problem.
elements.sent-2411-041: While there is flexibility in the form of the cost function, which can be adapted to various convex functions to meet specific needs, the direct application of linear MPC methods is often impractical.
elements.sent-2411-042: Inspired by online learning and nonlinear programming, we develop a novel learning-based single-shot multi-step prediction (SSMP) model paired with a corresponding NMPC strategy to tackle the three challenges outlined earlier in this work.
elements.sent-2411-043: Unlike traditional methods that incrementally construct a predictive horizon based on single-step predictions, our approach promotes direct multi-step prediction.
elements.sent-2411-044: This method effectively reduces the accumulation of errors and results in a more accurate model.
elements.sent-2411-045: Additionally, the SSMP framework minimizes the computational demands typically associated with neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-046: Our proposed predictive model leverages the strengths of both offline and online learning, effectively alleviating the burdens of online learning by building upon the robust foundation established during offline learning.
elements.sent-2411-047: This strategy enhances the model’s adaptability to dynamic environmental changes and significantly lowers the risk of uncontrolled behavior, which is a common issue in systems relying solely on online learning.
elements.sent-2411-048: We also account for approximation errors related to learning models within the NMPC.
elements.sent-2411-049: An adaptive parameter tuning method has been designed to address disturbances arising from model uncertainties, thus allowing us to obtain an optimized control sequence.
elements.sent-2411-050: Compared to state-of-the-art solutions, our approach is distinguished by its practical and economic advantages, particularly in applications such as the unmanned control of heavy-load hydraulic excavators.
elements.sent-2411-051: In summary, the main contributions of this work are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-052: Designing an SSMP neural network model based on long short-term memory (LSTM) and MLP to learn the nonlinear dynamics of robots.
elements.sent-2411-053: By predicting changes in states rather than the direct states, a marked enhancement can be achieved in the precision of the predictive model.
elements.sent-2411-054: The method for collecting data is also discussed.
elements.sent-2411-055: Combining offline and online models when robots interact with the external environment.
elements.sent-2411-056: Rather than directly modifying the parameters of the offline model, we employ a new MLP to learn and compensate for the mismatches.
elements.sent-2411-057: This approach helps us avoid the risk of losing control during interactions.
elements.sent-2411-058: Proposing an adaptive gradient descent optimization method for NMPC.
elements.sent-2411-059: We detail a cost function designed for multi-objective optimization and elucidate the derivation process for the predictive model.
elements.sent-2411-060: The impact of model fitting errors can be reduced by introducing the variable learning rate.
elements.sent-2411-061: Moreover, we use the average value of the control sequence instead of the first element.
elements.sent-2411-062: Validating the proposed methodology through simulation and experimentation on a heavy-load hydraulic excavator.
elements.sent-2411-063: The results demonstrate that the predictive model can adapt to complex nonlinear systems under various load conditions and achieve economic efficiency while meeting work requirements.
elements.sent-2411-064: The proposed method involves learning-based dynamics modeling and optimization for NMPC.
elements.sent-2411-065: We will begin with a brief overview of current learning models and training methods, followed by a discussion on solution methods for learning-based NMPC.
elements.sent-2411-066: Learning-based nonlinear dynamics model: Neural networks have emerged as a cutting-edge approach for capturing robotic systems' forward and inverse dynamics due to their universal approximation property.
elements.sent-2411-067: Our focus is on the neural network structures typically used for modeling forward dynamics, which include MLP \cite{cheng2015neural , deep belief networks (DBN) \cite{wang2020deep , radial basis function networks (RBF) \cite{chen2019rbfnn , physics-informed neural networks (PINN) \cite{karniadakis2021physics , LSTM networks \cite{huang2022lstm , and echo state networks (ESN) \cite{jordanou2021echo , among others.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: These learning models usually take the system's state variables and input signals as inputs, with data acquired through sensors.
elements.sent-2411-069: Most of these models are designed to learn dynamics in discrete time intervals.
elements.sent-2411-070: Provided that a sufficient and high-quality dataset is used, this approach keeps the model's predictive capability intact \cite{saviolo2023active .}
elements.sent-2411-071: The output of these models is flexible and can be tailored to meet specific needs.
elements.sent-2411-072: For example, a complete nonlinear system can be treated as a black box, allowing the neural network to fit the entire dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-073: Alternatively, prior knowledge about the physical mechanisms can be integrated to model part of the dynamics, which is commonly done in PINN.
elements.sent-2411-074: Unlike feedforward structures like MLP, RNNs are more complex and excel at extracting features from historical data, making them advantageous for time-series prediction and higher-order or time-delay nonlinear systems.
elements.sent-2411-075: Many predictive models provide single-step predictions, which can be extended iteratively to form a predictive horizon.
elements.sent-2411-076: In response, we have combined the strengths of LSTM and MLP to create the SSMP model, accompanied by a detailed discussion of its structure.
elements.sent-2411-077: The LSTM-MLP leverages the enhanced predictive capabilities of LSTM as an offline model, while MLP is employed as the online model due to its simpler training process.
elements.sent-2411-078: Learning method: Supervised training is typically used for the regression process, with the cost function defined as the norm of the prediction error \cite{lee2022precision .}
elements.sent-2411-079: Offline learning utilizes existing demonstration datasets, allowing for training without physical risks and minimizing the need for computational resources.
elements.sent-2411-080: However, this approach relies on high-quality datasets.
elements.sent-2411-081: When robots interact with their external environment, it is crucial to thoroughly consider the state across nearly all dimensions to capture the dynamic progression.
elements.sent-2411-082: Online learning \cite{bechtle2021leveraging ,\cite{waegeman2012feedback enables the synchronous updating of neural networks with refreshed data, but the learning process is fraught with uncertainties that can lead to control instability in real-time applications, particularly in robots that operate with high power and significant loads.}}
elements.sent-2411-083: This approach allows for model refinement by adjusting the weights of the last layer of the neural network through active learning.
elements.sent-2411-084: Our approach differs from traditional practices by conceptualizing the model as a blend of offline and online elements, similar to superimposing a robot's free and forced responses.
elements.sent-2411-085: This framework allows the system to quickly adapt to interactions, such as when a robot picks up an object.
elements.sent-2411-086: We effectively minimize associated risks by using the non-interactive state as a reference for action selection within NMPC.
elements.sent-2411-087: NMPC and optimization: NMPC primarily employs nonlinear predictive models and varies in solution methods.
elements.sent-2411-088: A common approach is to utilize linear MPC, which approximates the nonlinear objective function and constraints at each iteration as linear or quadratic functions.
elements.sent-2411-089: These approximations are then solved using QP or SQP.
elements.sent-2411-090: The ESN is utilized for free response and the forced response is obtained by a fast and recursive calculation of the input-output sensitivities from the ESN.
elements.sent-2411-091: While this approach is intuitive, the linearization process and repeated iterations can lead to a loss of predictive accuracy and increased demand for computational resources.
elements.sent-2411-092: An alternative approach to calculating the control sequence involves directly optimizing the cost function, utilizing evolutionary computation and gradient-based methods.
elements.sent-2411-093: Evolutionary computation methods, such as stochastic shooting \cite{saviolo2023active and differential evolution optimization \cite{zhang2021nonlinear , effectively manage complex constraints.}}
elements.sent-2411-094: However, these methods can be impractical for real-world applications due to their high computational resource requirements and time constraints.
elements.sent-2411-095: In contrast, direct computation of gradients within neural networks offers a more feasible solution, with optimization typically achieved through GD or the levenberg-marquardt (LM) algorithm, both of which are more universally applicable.
elements.sent-2411-096: When using gradient-based methods, setting a termination precision or a maximum number of iterations is expected to ensure a suboptimal solution.
elements.sent-2411-097: In \cite{cheng2015neural , an MLP is used to realize a nonlinear autoregressive moving average with exogenous inputs (NARMAX) model for a piezoelectric actuator.}
elements.sent-2411-098: The control sequence is obtained by solving the cost function using the LM algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-099: The final approach is the learning-based explicit NMPC \cite{drgovna2022differentiable ,\cite{kang2022tracking , where the optimal control sequence is computed through a neural network.}}
elements.sent-2411-100: The optimization process does not require iterations, significantly reducing the demand for computational resources.
elements.sent-2411-101: However, the offline-trained model lacks the flexibility to adapt to different tasks and accommodate variations in environmental interactions.
elements.sent-2411-102: In summary, directly computing the derivatives of neural networks and employing GD-based optimization methods require less computation and can achieve satisfactory results.

elements.sent-2411-106: Our application object is a hydraulic excavator, a heavy-duty mobile manipulation system powered by diesel fuel.
elements.sent-2411-107: This equipment is commonly used in construction, mining, waste management, and other heavy-load operations.
elements.sent-2411-108: With its excellent power-to-weight ratio and proven reliability, the hydraulic excavator currently has no viable substitutes, ensuring its continued significance in the industry for the foreseeable future.
elements.sent-2411-109: The hydraulic excavator is a complex assembly that comprises the following systems, as shown in Fig.\ref{hydraulic_excavator_introduction .}
elements.sent-2411-110: Power System: The power system of a hydraulic excavator is driven by a diesel engine.
elements.sent-2411-111: This engine operates piston pumps that deliver hydraulic fluid.
elements.sent-2411-112: It features near-constant torque regulation, which means the engine's power output is reflected in variations in speed, while the torque output remains relatively stable.
elements.sent-2411-113: As the throttle opening increases, the engine's output power also rises correspondingly.
elements.sent-2411-114: Hydraulic Drive System: The high-pressure oil produced by the hydraulic pump is transported to the actuator system through pipelines.
elements.sent-2411-115: To reduce fuel consumption and ensure optimal power matching, negative or positive flow hydraulic circuits are commonly used.
elements.sent-2411-116: Valve System: The direction or pressure of hydraulic fluid is adjusted through proportional solenoid valves, relief valves, and others.
elements.sent-2411-117: Actuation System: Including the left and right travel motors, the swing rotation motor, boom hydraulic cylinders, arm hydraulic cylinders, and bucket hydraulic cylinders, among others.
elements.sent-2411-118: Electronic Component System: It is responsible for converting signals into actions for the underlying electronic components.
elements.sent-2411-119: Furthermore, manufacturers typically incorporate basic functions in commercial products, such as low-power mode.

The final joint requires selecting the appropriate tool hand based on the task. To adapt to grasping tasks, we change to a clamping jaw.} \label{hydraulic_excavator_introduction}
elements.sent-2411-120: The unmanned operation of hydraulic excavators significantly reduces costs and eliminates the risk of injury to operators, thereby enhancing efficiency and safety.
elements.sent-2411-121: For a robot to perform engineering tasks automatically, it is essential to effectively control the power and actuation system.
elements.sent-2411-122: However, achieving high-quality automatic control of hydraulic excavators is a complex challenge.
elements.sent-2411-123: This complexity arises from the inherent nonlinearity of diesel engine and hydraulic drive systems and the strong coupling between the oil pump and the actuator within the positive flow system.
elements.sent-2411-124: This scenario involves nonlinear control and challenges related to power matching and energy scheduling.
elements.sent-2411-125: In addition, commercial hydraulic excavator products involve trade secrets, making it difficult to obtain complete structural information, which prevents the establishment of an accurate mathematical model.
elements.sent-2411-126: Therefore, we aim to address the unmanned control issue using data-driven methods, a currently prevalent approach in the field.
elements.sent-2411-127: In the following, we present the SSMP offline neural network model constructed utilizing LSTM and MLP, which is informed by Taylor expansion.
elements.sent-2411-128: We propose a prediction framework combining the offline and online models for enhanced performance.
elements.sent-2411-129: Finally, we discuss the cost function and optimization methods of NMPC.

elements.sent-2411-150: It is important to emphasize that we do not use the network to predict future states directly; instead, we focus on predicting the changes in states relative to the current moment.
elements.sent-2411-151: This approach offers several advantages because learning-based methods are inherently prone to prediction errors.
elements.sent-2411-152: When we attempt to make direct predictions about future states, the network's output may fluctuate around the actual values, potentially leading to significant errors that can cause predictions to stray from the original trend of state changes.
elements.sent-2411-153: On the other hand, predicting changes in states can help minimize fitting errors and improve the accuracy of trend predictions in state changes.
elements.sent-2411-154: The quality of data collection is essential for the learning model's predictive capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-155: We aim to obtain the sensitivity of the system's states to the input $U$ in the vicinity of each state point.
elements.sent-2411-156: The ideal data dimension should be as large as the $(n + m)$ state-input space of the system and the data range should be uniformly distributed across all accessible dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-157: However, collecting data this way is very labor-intensive, and achieving complete coverage of all dimensions presents significant challenges.
elements.sent-2411-158: Data collection strategies are generally classified into open-loop and closed-loop methods.
elements.sent-2411-159: The open-loop approach gathers system response data by applying random inputs.
elements.sent-2411-160: While this method is straightforward, it can raise safety concerns and often fails to adequately cover the entire state space.
elements.sent-2411-161: On the other hand, closed-loop techniques utilize predesigned controllers and trajectories, adjusting the trajectory profiles to cover each state point effectively.
elements.sent-2411-162: Although this approach minimizes the risks associated with operating outside of safe limits, it presents challenges.
elements.sent-2411-163: The data collected may still exhibit distributional bias in the multi-dimensional input space, which can result in skewed insights \cite{brunke2022safe .}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=3.5in]{data_collection.pdf} \caption{Data Collection Method. The workspace is a subset of the robot's reachable space. The safety monitor calculates the joint cartesian coordinates and determines whether it exceeds the workspace. The pre-configured controller is typically the controller provided by the manufacturer. } \label{data_collection}
elements.sent-2411-164: As illustrated in Fig.\ref{data_collection , we have integrated the two previously mentioned approaches and established safety protocols for data collection.}
elements.sent-2411-165: Since robots exhibit similar motion patterns when performing the same tasks, we determine their maximum operational workspace based on these tasks.
elements.sent-2411-166: We then gather data within this defined space using a combination of open-loop and closed-loop control strategies.
elements.sent-2411-167: This approach ensures that data collection is efficient and secure, leveraging the consistent motion characteristics observed in robots executing similar tasks.
elements.sent-2411-168: During the open-loop data collection phase, we introduce a sine input signal with a random magnitude and frequency within a specified range.
elements.sent-2411-169: The system continuously monitors its state to ensure it remains within the safe workspace.
elements.sent-2411-170: If any predefined threshold is exceeded, the input signal is promptly halted, and the system resets to the starting point.
elements.sent-2411-171: In the closed-loop collection phase, a preset sine trajectory is defined with random magnitude and frequency that fits within the operational workspace.
elements.sent-2411-172: This trajectory is tracked using a pre-configured controller, ensuring that the data collection process meets established safety and operational constraints.

elements.sent-2411-173: Robots frequently interact with their external environment while performing tasks such as transportation and grasping.
elements.sent-2411-174: These interactions can cause changes in the system's internal parameters, which may reduce the accuracy of the offline-trained predictive model.
elements.sent-2411-175: A decline in model accuracy can adversely impact the control performance of NMPC.
elements.sent-2411-176: Therefore, it is crucial to account for these interaction dynamics to ensure the effectiveness and reliability of robotic control systems across various operational contexts.
elements.sent-2411-177: In response to the variations, traditional methods often rely on sensors to measure external parameters or treat them as disturbances, adjusting inputs accordingly to minimize their effects.
elements.sent-2411-178: However, in many engineering situations, inadequate sensors can hinder robots from accurately detecting the precise impacts of their interactions with the external environment.
elements.sent-2411-179: Drawing inspiration from free and forced response processes, we view the offline-trained model as representing the system's nominal behavior without external interference.
elements.sent-2411-180: In contrast, interactive processes introduce unknown disturbances, leading to a forced response from the robots.
elements.sent-2411-181: This perspective allows robots to adapt to unexpected changes and maintain optimal performance.
elements.sent-2411-182: As a result, we have developed an online learning approach designed to detect and accommodate variations.
elements.sent-2411-183: This approach combines an online learning neural network model with the existing offline-trained model, aiming to enhance the accuracy of model predictions in real time.
elements.sent-2411-184: We initially train a basic offline model without external interaction.
elements.sent-2411-185: During the task, when the robot faces environmental changes or experiences varying loads, the accuracy of the offline model becomes compromised.
elements.sent-2411-186: Then, we record the actual state $X$ and the forward simulation $X_hat$ of the offline model throughout the task execution process and define the mismatch as ${{X _{error =X-\hat{X}$ which can reflect the changes in the current system model relative to the no-load model.}}}
elements.sent-2411-187: We employ an MLP to facilitate online training $H$, as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq5} \begin{aligned} {{\hat{X}}_{error}}{{\ }_{t+1:t+i}}=H(X_{t}^{h},U_{t-1}^{h},{{U}_{t:t+i-1}};{{w}_{H}},{{b}_{H}}) \end{aligned}\end{equation} Compared to the serial structure of LSTM, training an MLP is less computationally intensive, which accelerates the convergence process.
elements.sent-2411-188: This advantage makes the MLP particularly useful in situations requiring quick model updates.
elements.sent-2411-189: The online model maintains the same input and output specifications as the offline model, ensuring continuity and ease of implementation.
elements.sent-2411-190: The training dataset is generated from historical data collected during the system's online operations, enabling the model to learn from past experiences and improve its predictions.
elements.sent-2411-191: For instance, we calculate the error between the historical actual state $\left[ \begin{matrix {{X _{t-h {{X _{t-h+1 \ldots {{X _{t \end{matrix \right]$ and the prediction state $\left[ \begin{matrix {{{\hat{X _{t-h {{{\hat{X _{t-h+1 \ldots {{{\hat{X _{t \end{matrix \right]$ from the offline model at time $t$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-192: After obtaining the error, we employ stochastic gradient descent (SGD) to iteratively refine the weights $w$ and biases $b$ of the MLP in an online setting to minimize the error.
elements.sent-2411-193: The formula is as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq6} \begin{aligned} M=\left\| X_{t}^{h}-\hat{X}_{t}^{h}-{{{\hat{X}}}_{error}}\ _{t}^{h} \right\|_{2}^{2} {{w}_{H}}(k)={{w}_{H}}(k-1)-\frac{{{\eta }_{w}}}{B}\sum\limits_{i=k-B}^{k}{\frac{\partial M}{\partial U}} {{b}_{H}}(k)={{b}_{H}}(k-1)-\frac{{{\eta }_{b}}}{B}\sum\limits_{i=k-B}^{k}{\frac{\partial M}{\partial U}} \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $k$ is the current iteration and $B$ is batch size.
elements.sent-2411-194: Learning rate ${{\eta _{w }$ and ${{\eta _{b }$ are hyperparameters that determine the adaptability of the online model.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-195: Starting with small initial weights and biases is essential for every online learning process.
elements.sent-2411-196: This cautious approach helps the model avoid the interference of errors caused by random initial parameters.
elements.sent-2411-197: Employing mini-batch training enhances the stability of the training process and improves the generalization of the results.
elements.sent-2411-198: Industrial robots often function as complex, MIMO-coupled nonlinear systems.
elements.sent-2411-199: Their controllers are designed to meet the demands of production and manufacturing tasks.
elements.sent-2411-200: We also aim to enhance the robots' energy efficiency, as this can result in significant economic savings, which is crucial for the economic viability and sustainability of industrial automation.
elements.sent-2411-201: NMPC is particularly effective in control systems that involve multi-objective optimization.
elements.sent-2411-202: It utilizes a specified nonlinear system dynamics model to minimize a cost function, denoted as $J$, which helps extract the most effective sequence of control inputs.
elements.sent-2411-203: This cost function accounts for the system's inherent constraints and desired behaviors.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{NMPC}\label{NMPC_algorithm} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE Train the offline learned model based on motion data; \STATE Initial the online learning model; \STATE While task do \STATE Input: System state $X$; \STATE For $i\ in\ [1:k_1]$ do \STATE Set the $U=0$, iteration = $i$ \STATE Predict the system output $\hat{Y}$ \STATE Calculate jacobian matrix \STATE Update control signal through GD \STATE Record the input and output data; \STATE For $j\ in\ [1:k_2]$ do \STATE Calculate the error of prediction \STATE Error backpropagation and update online model \STATE Output: Control signal $U$; \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-229: Through NMPC, we aim to achieve a balance between industrial robots' work performance and energy efficiency to reduce production and manufacturing costs.
elements.sent-2411-230: To assess the efficacy of our proposed approach, we carry out simulations and practical experiments with a hydraulic excavator.
elements.sent-2411-231: In the following, we will provide a detailed introduction to experiments and simulations involving the hydraulic excavator.
elements.sent-2411-232: Our analysis focuses on a specific model of a 22-ton hydraulic excavator.
elements.sent-2411-233: To enhance our understanding of its operational dynamics, we have developed a simplified simulation model targeting the manipulators using AMESim and Simulink.
elements.sent-2411-234: As illustrated in Fig.\ref{Simulation , we employ a variable-displacement piston pump to supply hydraulic fluid to three actuators, based on a hydraulic excavator demonstration in AMESim, effectively replicating the oil supply circuitry.}
elements.sent-2411-235: Furthermore, we consider a positive flow system, characterized by control signals that simultaneously influence multiple solenoid proportional valves and the variable displacement pump.
elements.sent-2411-236: This system manages both the opening of the proportional valves and the pump's output flow rate, closely resembling the operation of the actual machine.
elements.sent-2411-237: We also incorporate common nonlinear characteristics, such as dead zones and leakage, into our model.
elements.sent-2411-238: The control targets include the engine speed and the electromagnetic proportional valves for the three primary actuators of the hydraulic excavator.
elements.sent-2411-239: In the actual machine, the engine is equipped with ten forward gears.
elements.sent-2411-240: To accommodate various work tasks, we select the third, fifth, and seventh gears to represent the simulation model's low, medium, and high operational ranges.
elements.sent-2411-241: We have omitted the inertia related to adjusting the engine's speed because gear shifts can be performed at will and are completed almost instantaneously in the actual machinery.
elements.sent-2411-242: Additionally, a relief valve is connected to the pump's outlet, serving as a safety feature, and the overflow can be used to determine wasted energy.

elements.sent-2411-369: Robots' real-time control requires a certain frequency of motion commands, thus minimizing the computation time for each NMPC iteration.
elements.sent-2411-370: Since the predictive model is SSMP, the computational complexity of the neural network is influenced by LSTM cycles, input dimensions, layers, neurons, activation function, and prediction length.
elements.sent-2411-371: Assuming a single layer LSTM cycle count of $h$, state dimension of $n$, and hidden neurons of $j$, the operations include the input gate, forget gate, and output gate, as well as the computations for the hidden states and cell units.
elements.sent-2411-372: The computational complexity can be approximated as $O(4jh(n+j+3))$ .
elements.sent-2411-373: The MLP has an input signal dimension of $m$, hidden neurons of $j$, with a single hidden layer, and a predictive horizon $N$.
elements.sent-2411-374: The process involves multiple matrix multiplications, and the computational complexity can be approximated as $O((m+1)j+(j+1)j+(N+1)j)$.
elements.sent-2411-375: Thus, the computational complexity of the entire framework can be approximated as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq15} \begin{aligned} O(4jh(n+j+3))+O((hn+2m+3j+2N+6)j) \end{aligned}\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-382: In response to the challenges of automation in unmanned industrial robotics, this paper proposes a data-driven NMPC framework that primarily addresses 1) accurate predictive models of nonlinear dynamics, 2) robot-environment interactions, and 3) universal solution methods.
elements.sent-2411-383: The proposed NMPC approach allows for customizing cost functions in convex form, aligning with various engineering tasks particularly adept at handling energy management requirements.
elements.sent-2411-384: This holds significant promise for industrial applications, as it stands to enhance economic performance.
elements.sent-2411-385: First, we introduce the design process of the SSMP model.
elements.sent-2411-386: Based on Taylor expansion, the predictive horizon can be obtained from historical states and input sequences, prompting us to employ LSTM and MLP to construct an incremental predictive model.
elements.sent-2411-387: The proposed model outperforms standard MLP in multi-step prediction.
elements.sent-2411-388: Safety is considered by adopting a combination of open-loop and closed-loop methods in data collection, with the range determined by work tasks.
elements.sent-2411-389: Furthermore, to address model mismatches caused by the robot's interaction with the external environment, an online model is introduced to learn the variation.
elements.sent-2411-390: The results demonstrate that this online model significantly enhances the robot's predictive accuracy post-engagement with loads.
elements.sent-2411-391: This hybrid offline-online strategy also mitigates the risk of losing control.
elements.sent-2411-392: Finally, we choose a GD-based NMPC solution method and set an adaptive learning rate to mitigate the impact of model prediction errors.
elements.sent-2411-393: Such an optimization method applies to most convex function forms of cost functions and is highly flexible.
elements.sent-2411-394: We conduct simulations and experiments on a 22-ton hydraulic excavator, employing a cost function to simultaneously control the multiple joints and the diesel engine.
elements.sent-2411-395: The results show that by adjusting the excavator's output power, we can increase fuel economy and safety.
elements.sent-2411-396: It is worth noting that hydraulic excavators encompass both power and actuation systems, and our approach can be extended to general industrial systems that involve energy generation and utilization.
elements.sent-2411-397: It is capable of addressing nonlinear challenges and achieving ideal energy distribution.
elements.sent-2411-398: Currently, our approach is purely data-driven, without considering the impact of complex noise, and has yet to undergo detailed theoretical validation.
elements.sent-2411-399: The future works encompass the following: 1) developing a hybrid predictive model that combines data with physics to reduce the burden of data collection, 2) addressing data processing in the presence of complex noise, 3) validating simulation and experimental results through comprehensive theoretical analysis, and 4) transforming our approach into commercial software.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran .}
